text: In light of the immense potential of hydrogen as a clean energy carrier, the widespread adoption of hydrogen fuel cells for clean energy conversion and utilization is becoming a highly anticipated development in the new energy era. The cathodic oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in hydrogen fuel cells is, however, notoriously sluggish, a hindrance that, in conjunction with other kinetic factors, has prohibited the economic commercialization of fuel cells despite nearly five decades of relentless research and technological innovation, largely owing to the high cost of Pt or platinum-group metal catalysts required for the cathode. Herein, an atomically dispersed bimetal single metal catalyst called Zn/Co-N-C, derived from a bimetallic cobalt- zinc metal organic framework (MOF) composed of Co-N4 and Zn-N4 structures is designed to achieve very low cost and maximize activity. After optimization in alkaline solution, the half wave potential reached 0.938 V at the cathode, a value record high for single-atom nonprecious-metal ORR catalysts in alkaline solution to date and the catalyst also exhibits outstanding durability characterized by losing merely 8.5 mV after 50,000 cycles. As prepared, as well as membrane electrode assembly (MEA) catalysts give a record peak power density of approximately one Watts per square centimeter at the lowest cobalt loading in an AEMFC. UtilizingoperandoXAS it is demonstrated that Co-N4 sites in the structure are the active centers and an implicit potential dependent activity model was set up based on the edgeshift of XAS spectra for revealing bonding relationship between intermediates and catalyst surfaces at different potentials. Calculations revealed that Co-N4 centers favored intermediate adsorption even at high potential due to thermodynamic factors, with detailed atomistic bonding sites quantitatively estimated via core level shifts of XAS spectra and further support the proposed mechanism with enhanced comprehensiveness for realizing the potential dependent activity of Zn/Co-N-C catalysts. The Zn/Co-N-C as a novel and complex single atom catalyst offers a further understanding of the design concept, function and optimization route of dual-metal catalysts in energy applications.
